summary: This notebook implements a high-throughput inference pipeline for the AIMO 3 competition using the gpt-oss-120b model served via vLLM. It demonstrates a robust tool-augmented reasoning framework where the LLM dynamically executes Python code in isolated Jupyter kernels to verify calculations and explore solutions. The author implements a parallel multi-attempt generation strategy with dynamic time budgeting and entropy-weighted answer selection to improve solution reliability. Key takeaways include effective strategies for managing GPU memory, orchestrating persistent sandbox environments for tool use, and leveraging token-level uncertainty to filter and aggregate probabilistic outputs.
competition_flow: Loads test problems via the official Kaggle inference server wrapper, processes them through a parallelized vLLM-served LLM with Python tool-augmented reasoning, and returns formatted integer answers.
data_processing: Uses Harmony encoding for tokenization and applies a custom chat template with system prompts, tool configurations, and mathematical reasoning instructions.
ensembling: Generates multiple parallel reasoning attempts per problem and aggregates results using an entropy-weighted voting scheme that prioritizes answers from low-entropy (high-confidence) generations.
insights: Persistent Jupyter kernel pools drastically reduce overhead for tool-augmented LLM reasoning compared to spawning new processes per call.
Token-level entropy serves as a reliable proxy for model confidence, enabling effective answer filtering without external validators.
Dynamic time budgeting based on remaining problems and early stopping on consensus prevents timeout failures in long-running inference loops.
